content: Currently Task has an unsafe impl Sync but it is not Send. Is that intentional? Is there some reason it can't also be Send?
Binder currently stores an ARef<Task>, which assumes that Task is Send.
a bit unrelated to your question, but are there any good resources for understanding when a structure can implement Sync and Send? (apart from send-and-sync)

There are three relevant permissions:

  1. Access value mutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization. (Send gives this.)
  2. Access value immutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization. (Send and Sync both gives this - you only need one of them to have it.)
  3. Access value immutably from threads other than the one it was created on, without synchronization. (Sync gives this.)
when you say "with synchronization", do you mean the synchronization is provided to you when you access that structure?
No, I mean that the access must be synchronized externally. That is, the access must happen one thread at the time.

Anyway, I went ahead and submitted it upstream. You can give a review there if there's a problem with Task being Send.
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20230517095905.3548100-2-aliceryhl@google.com/

Alice Ryhl said:

There are three relevant permissions:

  1. Access value mutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization. (Send gives this.)
  2. Access value immutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization. (Send and Sync both gives this - you only need one of them to have it.)
  3. Access value immutably from threads other than the one it was created on, without synchronization. (Sync gives this.)

I always think of it as send -> it is safe to send the owned value to another thread, sync -> it is safe to send a reference to another thread. This implies that you don't always need synchronization for 1) and that 2) can give mutable access with interior mutability. Anyway @Ariel Miculas this is a good reference.

When I said "with synchronization", what I meant is just that you don't have several threads doing it at the same time in parallel. As for interior mutability, I use the words mutable and immutable to refer to whether the methods are &self or &mut self, so interior mutability should be interpreted as immutable.

Under the above interpretations of my words, it should be precise.

Alice Ryhl said:

Currently Task has an unsafe impl Sync but it is not Send. Is that intentional? Is there some reason it can't also be Send?

Looking at the code, it seems that sync is in place to make TaskRef send. Rust code should not own a Task and thus it makes no difference if it is send or not.
Except TaskRef is explicitly !send so then never mind :shrug:
Binder owns an ARef<Task> in some places.
But still, TaskRef is the only way to obtain a Task handle, you would never hold an owned Task? So send does not matter.
(That's also why I submitted it together with an ARef patch.)

Alice Ryhl said:

Binder owns an ARef<Task> in some places.

But that is a reference to a Task, so send for Task does not matter in this case?
Arc requires both Send and Sync because mutable access happens when the refcount hits zero.
If Task is sync then ARef<Task> should be send. At least nothing in the relationship to the referenced value should stop it from being send

Alice Ryhl said:

Arc requires both Send and Sync because mutable access happens when the refcount hits zero.

But this is unrelated to ARef ?
ARef is essentially an Arc, so I used the same safety requirements.
You could also add a safety requirement to the AlwaysRefcountedTrait saying that if the type is Sync, then it must be ok for the refcount to hit zero on any thread, even if the value is not Send.

Alice Ryhl said:

You could also add a safety requirement to the AlwaysRefcountedTrait saying that if the type is Sync, then it must be ok for the refcount to hit zero on any thread, even if the value is not Send.

I am not certain, but I believe that it is OK for the C struct task_struct to hit zero refcount on any thread. That is, the C side does not have the requirement you are describing.
Normally, the way you would encode that is by making the type Send.

I think what the C people do if they have a refcount_t that they send around but the value must be freed in a specific local context, the will queue the free on a queue to be picked up later and executed in that specific context.
That makes sense.

To me, the reason that I submitted the patch is that ARef<Task> should be Send/Sync. Since it is very similar to Arc, I reused how they justify that this is ok, which involves making the inner type Send. I am open to hearing other suggestions for achieving the same thing.

It is difficult for me to reason about send in this particular case, because struct task_struct is never owned. If it was owned, it would be Pin. And a Pin<Task> would be send. It' just feels like an odd exercise, since one would never have that type.
Fundamentally, Send is about where mutable access may happen, and not about ownership.
Mutable access to Task with synchronization would be OK. I cannot find a reason it would not be.
Synchronization meaning proper serialization
Then it should be send.
What patch are you referring to?
I'm a little behind

I linked it earlier:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20230517095905.3548100-2-aliceryhl@google.com/
Looks good to me
Great, thanks for taking a look!
Looks like I have to refine my mental model for send though :laughing:
yeah, it's very non-obvious, especially because of how people usually use ownership when talking about it

I'm trying to wrap my head around Send (from here):

Almost every Rust type is Send, but there are some exceptions, including Rc<T>: this cannot be Send because if you cloned an Rc<T> value
and tried to transfer ownership of the clone to another thread, both threads might update the reference count at the same time.

And @Alice Ryhl said

When I said "with synchronization", what I meant is just that you don't have several threads doing it at the same time in parallel.

These statements seem contradictory, I think I'm missing something in my understanding.
@Ariel Miculas If you have two different Rc<T> objects, then accessing them in parallel from two different threads would be a data race. This doesn't violate rule 1 because it's not the same object.

Ariel Miculas said:

Alice Ryhl said

When I said "with synchronization", what I meant is just that you don't have several threads doing it at the same time in parallel.

These statements seem contradictory, I think I'm missing something in my understanding.

It's true that synchronization might not be the best word to use here. Synchronization is one way to ensure that threads don't access the same thing in parallel, but its not the only way you can ensure that.
So the rules don't technically prevent Rc<T> from being Send, but if it was marked Send then it could lead to data races, so for this reason it's not marked Send. Did I get it right?
or does it break rule 1 because it doesn't use synchronization when updating its reference counter?
The rules prevent Rc<T> from being Send in the sense that _if_ it was Send, then you could cause a data race in safe code. Any API that can cause a data race in safe code is incorrect.
Any change to the API that would make that data race impossible in safe code would make the API correct again. Removing the impl Send is one such API change.
thanks, I'm starting to understand now :smiley:
Having it use atomics for the refcount instead would also be such an API change. (That's how you get the Arc.)
yeah, that makes sense

Ariel Miculas said:

or does it break rule 1 because it doesn't use synchronization when updating its reference counter?

To expand on this, I don't think its meaningful to say that the type itself can break these rules. From the perspective of external code, they are rules that the external code must follow (that is, it makes sense to talk about whether external code breaks or follows them.) However, from the perspective of the implementation of the type, the rules are not rules, but permissions that the implementation gives to unknown user code. You can talk about whether it is incorrect to give out that permission, but you can't really talk about breaking a permission per se when you are the one who gives out the permission.

Most uses of raw pointers should be encapsulated behind a sufficient abstraction that Send and Sync can be derived.
So if I encapsulate a raw pointer in my structure, the only case when I cannot implement Sync and Send for it is if the users of my structure could cause UB in safe rust (e.g. through a data race)?
Yeah, basically, as long as the API you provide cannot be used to trigger UB, then it is ok.
And as you quoted, implementing Send/Sync is usually ok.

I have this in my API and I kept wondering if I can implement Send/Sync:

    pub fn get(&self) -> *mut bindings::vfsmount {
        self.vfsmount
    }

but I guess I'll just blame the users who will deal with the raw pointer in their unsafe code.

Alice Ryhl said:

yeah, it's very non-obvious, especially because of how people usually use ownership when talking about it

Hmm.. but

Access value mutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization.

means the other threads can move the value which may end up freeing/dropping the value in another thread that is not the creator of the value. So "ownership" mental model still makes sense then?
Which thread owns a value while it's in an Arc?
Which thread owns a value while it's in a global variable?
These kinds of tricky questions is why the ownership model is tricky.
Arc describes a shared ownership, if the refcount is 1 and we have a &mut Arc<..>, then we owns the value.

Alice Ryhl said:

Which thread owns a value while it's in a global variable?

Do you have an example that a global variable is Send?

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Which thread owns a value while it's in a global variable?

Do you have an example that a global variable is Send?

Are you asking for something like this? 

static MY_GLOBAL: u32 = 10;

Alice Ryhl said:

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Which thread owns a value while it's in a global variable?

Do you have an example that a global variable is Send?

Are you asking for something like this? 

static MY_GLOBAL: u32 = 10;

Ah, that's fair, let me think more.
I guess the question should be when you need a global variable to be Send.
As for ownership, global variables are owned by the threads creating them.
I mean, a static that isn't marked mut doesn't need to be Send. Mutable access to it will never happen, not even a destructor.

Alice Ryhl said:

These kinds of tricky questions is why the ownership model is tricky.

I don't think there is a fundamental difference between the ownership model and your model. for me &mut Self means ownership.
The reason I prefer my model is because it gives a better intuition. I'm sure you can make both models work formally.

Hmm.. look again, I'm not getting why the "with synchronization" part is needed:

  1. Access value mutably from threads other than the one it was created on, with synchronization. (Send gives this.)

, since when you access the value mutably, you have a &mut reference, and you can only get it safely with some synchronization, so mutably implies synchronization I guess.
Yes, there's no option that allows mutable access without synchronization.
Thanks! BTW, I agree that Task can be Send.

Alice Ryhl said:

The reason I prefer my model is because it gives a better intuition. I'm sure you can make both models work formally.

To me, the ownership model gives the intuition that a function requiring Send is usually a "sending point" of a type to give the ownership to another thread. And that thread may call the drop function of the type.

Yeah, I think the reason I dislike the ownership model is because it implies some sort of "event" where ownership is transferred as an action. And then things like Arc::get_mut_unchecked would have to perform that action before giving you a reference, even though the sending action is really a no-op and doesn't appear in the code at all.
